description: Exclude-rule confidence tiers for code-graph scan scope, built from iteration 5.

high.definition: Infrastructure, dependency caches, interpreter caches, runtime indexes, or metadata paths with near-zero source-authority value for code graph answers.
high.default_action: exclude_without_prompt; allow explicit include override for narrow fixture/debug investigations

patterns.rationale: Git metadata is traversal noise and already hard-excluded by EXCLUDED_FOR_CODE_GRAPH.
false_positive_examples: Throwaway Git repositories in tests may contain .git directories, but those internals are runtime fixture metadata rather than source symbols.

patterns.rationale: Installed dependency trees are reproducible package-manager output and already excluded by scanner defaults plus root ignore rules.
false_positive_examples: Package-manager fixture tests may intentionally model node_modules trees; use explicit fixture include overrides.

patterns.rationale: Python bytecode cache; source authority is the .py file.
false_positive_examples: Cleanup/ignore-rule test fixtures may intentionally include __pycache__ path samples; include only the fixture, not all interpreter caches.

patterns.rationale: Pytest runtime cache, not structural source.
false_positive_examples: Cache contents may aid debugging but should not drive symbol graph answers.

patterns.rationale: Local Python environment/dependency install, analogous to node_modules.
false_positive_examples: Tutorial repos may mention virtualenv layout, but committed source should live outside the environment directory.

patterns.rationale: Local Python dependency environment; indexing it would fold third-party installed packages into the workspace graph.
false_positive_examples: Legacy tutorials may commit a minimal venv-like fixture; include explicitly if that fixture is the target.

patterns.rationale: OS metadata file, not code.
false_positive_examples: Ignore-rule or file-sanitization tests may include a .DS_Store fixture path; include explicitly if that metadata behavior is the subject.

patterns.rationale: Local semantic-index database/cache, not source for code-graph symbols.
false_positive_examples: Inspecting CocoIndex internals is a separate database/debug task, not default code-graph indexing.

medium.definition: Conventional build outputs, vendored trees, generated trees, or output folders that are often noise but have common real-world source/test exceptions.
medium.default_action: exclude_by_default_for_general_scans; surface before adding new global defaults

patterns.rationale: Usually compiled/package output, but distribution fixtures and checked-in browser/package bundles can be meaningful regression context.
false_positive_examples: Open-source package/test fixtures sometimes keep built distribution trees; dist means distribution, not always disposable source.

patterns.rationale: Often build output, but segment-style matching can hit legitimate source package names.
false_positive_examples: Bazel has canonical Java source under src/main/java/com/google/devtools/build.

patterns.rationale: Often output, but expected-output fixtures and APIs with 'out' naming can be test-critical.
false_positive_examples: PyTorch has committed test_out_dtype_op.py source-like regression coverage around out APIs.

patterns.rationale: Vendored dependencies are often bloat, but may be required for dependency graph fidelity.
false_positive_examples: Kubernetes commits a large vendor tree with Go module source and vendor/modules.txt.

patterns.rationale: This repo treats external as temporary/non-canonical, but monorepos can use external integrations as source inputs.
false_positive_examples: Bazel/TensorFlow-style ecosystems may expose external repositories or wrappers under external-like paths.

patterns.rationale: Generated code is often derivable noise but can be the checked-in public API/client surface.
false_positive_examples: Generated API/client repositories can commit generated sources as the implementation users consume.

low.definition: Repo-local names or lifecycle folders whose meaning is project policy, not ecosystem convention.

patterns.rationale: Fixtures are often intentionally committed and semantically useful; iteration 4 warned not to globally exclude fixtures except under known excluded roots.
false_positive_examples: ESLint commits tests/fixtures as test context.
Go commits src/cmd/go/testdata files.

patterns.rationale: Language ecosystems commonly use testdata as committed regression material, not generated output.
false_positive_examples: Go's src/cmd/go/testdata contains committed files needed for tests.

patterns.rationale: Scratch can mean throwaway work, imported sample repos, or active investigation material depending on repo policy.
false_positive_examples: This repo has scratch paths under spec archives/templates that may be useful for migration or historical debugging.

patterns.rationale: Repo lifecycle convention, not ecosystem-wide generated/noise signal.
false_positive_examples: This repo's z_archive contains prior spec packets and scratch repos that can be important historical context.

patterns.rationale: Repo lifecycle convention for future/experimental work; can contain real source-like prototypes and research packets.
false_positive_examples: This repo's z_future contains agentic-system and hybrid-RAG research packets.

patterns.rationale: Repo-specific ignored product spec trees; useful when the operator targets those product areas.
false_positive_examples: This repo has .opencode/specs/00--ai-systems, .opencode/specs/00--anobel.com, and .opencode/specs/00--barter.

patterns.rationale: Root ignore rules mark this as not part of the framework, but it is a local project-area decision.
false_positive_examples: This repo's working tree includes /ai_systems as a real project directory for non-framework tasks.
